planting.note: Direct sow seeds in early spring in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; blazingstar has sticky leaves that trap insects — handle with care. It does not transplant well and should always be sown in place. The flowers open in the afternoon and early evening, releasing their fragrance for moth pollinators as dusk approaches.

directions: Direct sow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun; Lindley's blazingstar is one of California's most beautiful native annual wildflowers — producing large (2–3 inch), golden-yellow, five-petaled flowers of exceptional saturation and brilliance, each with a prominent central boss of numerous golden stamens that gives the flower a sunburst quality on a par with the finest rose. The flowers open in the afternoon (remaining closed during morning hours) and emit a rich, warm, vanilla-honey fragrance at dusk that attracts hawkmoths and other night-flying pollinators. The leaves and stems are covered with tiny, barbed hairs that stick to clothing and trap small insects — an adaptation shared with the genus. Native to dry, rocky, sandy habitats in the California Coast Ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills, it thrives in lean, well-drained soils where competing vegetation is sparse. In the garden, mass sowings create a brilliant field of golden flowers in spring and early summer, particularly in gravel gardens and dry native plant meadows. The evening-opening, fragrant flowers make it an exceptional plant for any area where an outdoor seating space or evening garden can capture the dusk fragrance.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in early spring or fall (in mild climates) in lean, well-drained soil in full sun; cream cups is a cool-season annual that performs best before summer heat and self-sows to maintain colonies in lean, open soils. It does not transplant well; always direct sow. Best sown in broad drifts for the most attractive meadow effect.

directions: Direct sow seeds in cool weather in lean to moderately fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; cream cups is a delicate California native annual, the sole species in its genus, producing grayish-hairy, narrow leaves on spreading stems and in spring (February through May), a succession of small (1 inch), six-petaled, cream-to-pale-yellow poppy flowers of the softest, most delicate, barely-there coloring — the petals creamy-white to very pale butter-yellow, almost translucent in full sun, giving each flower a fresh, light, candlelight quality. In mass plantings or in natural meadow settings with other California native annuals, the cream-colored flowers create a soft, light, airy effect quite different from the stronger colors of California poppy or gilia. It is one of the few California native annuals in this soft, cream-to-pale-yellow range rather than the intense oranges, reds, and blues of other species. Outstanding in native wildflower meadow mixes as a soft foil to bolder-colored companions, and in gravel gardens and the front of lean-soil borders where its modest stature and soft color work well. Native bees forage the flowers readily.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in lean, very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nuttall's linanthus is native to dry, rocky, chaparral and coastal sage scrub in California and Baja California and requires excellent drainage. It is perennial in zones 8–10 but may be grown as an annual in colder regions. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain compact form.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Nuttall's linanthus is a California and Baja California native subshrub of the chaparral, producing exceptionally fine-textured, needle-like leaves that give the entire plant a distinctive, soft, feathery texture strongly reminiscent of heather or fine pine foliage — creating year-round textural interest before the plant flowers. In spring and early summer it produces small, tubular, five-petaled flowers in white to pale lavender carried in tight clusters at the branch tips, and on a mature, well-established plant, these flower clusters can be produced in sufficient abundance to create an attractive display against the distinctive, very fine, needle-textured foliage. Native to dry, rocky, chaparral habitats of the California Coast Ranges where it grows among other drought-adapted shrubs, it is well adapted to the Mediterranean-climate conditions of California and Gulf Coast gardens. The fine, needle-like foliage texture provides year-round structural interest in the dry garden that few other plants can match. Outstanding combined with other California native shrubs — ceanothus, manzanita, and salvias — in low-water native plant gardens.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant after danger of frost passes in very well-drained, lean, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; rock purslane is perennial in zones 9–11 and extremely drought-tolerant once established. In colder zones grow as a tender annual from seed. The succulent, gray-green foliage provides attractive texture even when not in bloom.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; rock purslane is a Chilean native succulent perennial producing rosettes of thick, fleshy, glaucous gray-green succulent leaves and from spring through fall, slender, wiry stems bearing flowers of an astonishingly vivid, pure, hot fuchsia-magenta color — one of the most intense and eye-catching flower colors achievable in the drought-tolerant garden. Each individual bloom is a simple, five-petaled cup of brilliant, saturated magenta-cerise on a delicate stem, and the contrast of these vivid flowers against the cool, gray-green succulent foliage is exceptional. It is more drought-tolerant than most flowering plants with such saturated color, native to the dry, rocky coastal hills of Chile where it forms sprawling mats over exposed rocky ground. In zones 9–11 it grows as a substantial, semi-woody, spreading perennial that blooms for much of the year. In colder zones treat as a tender annual from early-started seed. Outstanding in containers, rock gardens, and dry stone wall plantings where both the fleshy foliage and vivid flowers provide interest, and where drought tolerance is essential.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost or direct sow after soil warms to 70°F in full sun; wingpod purslane requires no watering once established in sandy or gravelly soil. Unlike moss rose (P. grandiflora), the flowers of P. umbraticola (used to breed most modern Portulaca hybrids) do not close in cloudy weather — an important horticultural advantage. Most nursery 'Portulaca' sold as 'Sundial', 'Yubi', or 'Pazzazz' series are derived from or are this species.

directions: Plant in lean, very well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; wingpod purslane is a heat-loving succulent annual native to the southern United States and tropical America that is the primary parent species behind most modern portulaca hybrids sold under names like 'Sundial', 'Yubi', and 'Pazzazz.' It produces succulent, cylindrical to flat, glossy, bright-green leaves on sprawling, succulent stems and from early summer through frost, vivid, tissue-paper-textured flowers (1–2 inches across) in the full range of hot portulaca colors — crimson, scarlet, magenta, orange, yellow, white, and bicolors — that, importantly, stay open longer in cloudy conditions than the related moss rose (P. grandiflora), making them a better choice for regions with overcast summer weather. Absolutely the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of all colorful summer annuals, thriving in pure sand or gravel with no supplemental irrigation in the hottest summer conditions. Outstanding for the tops of dry stone walls, sandy road edges, the front edge of gravel driveways, and any impossible, hot, dry location where virtually nothing else will flower. An essential plant for Gulf Coast and zone 9 summer gardens where heat and drought defeat most annuals.
